chapter.p: The library looked as she had pictured it. The green-shaded lamps made tranquil circles of light in the gathering dusk, a little fire flickered on the hearth, and Selden‚Äôs easy-chair, which stood near it, had been pushed aside when he rose to admit her.
He had checked his first movement of surprise, and stood silent, waiting for her to speak, while she paused a moment on the threshold, assailed by a rush of memories.

The scene was unchanged. She recognized the row of shelves from which he had taken down his La Bruy√®re, and the worn arm of the chair he had leaned against while she examined the precious volume. But then the wide September light had filled the room, making it seem a part of the outer world: now the shaded lamps and the warm hearth, detaching it from the gathering darkness of the street, gave it a sweeter touch of intimacy.

Becoming gradually aware of the surprise under Selden‚Äôs silence, Lily turned to him and said simply: ‚ÄúI came to tell you that I was sorry for the way we parted‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor what I said to you that day at Mrs. Hatch‚Äôs.‚Äù

The words rose to her lips spontaneously. Even on her way up the stairs, she had not thought of preparing a pretext for her visit, but she now felt an intense longing to dispel the cloud of misunderstanding that hung between them.

Selden returned her look with a smile. ‚ÄúI was sorry too that we should have parted in that way; but I am not sure I didn‚Äôt bring it on myself. Luckily I had foreseen the risk I was taking‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù
‚ÄúSo that you really didn‚Äôt care‚ÅÝ‚Äî?‚Äù broke from her with a flash of her old irony.

‚ÄúSo that I was prepared for the consequences,‚Äù he corrected good-humouredly. ‚ÄúBut we‚Äôll talk of all this later. Do come and sit by the fire. I can recommend that armchair, if you‚Äôll let me put a cushion behind you.‚Äù

While he spoke she had moved slowly to the middle of the room, and paused near his writing-table, where the lamp, striking upward, cast exaggerated shadows on the pallor of her delicately-hollowed face.
‚ÄúYou look tired‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo sit down,‚Äù he repeated gently.
She did not seem to hear the request. ‚ÄúI wanted you to know that I left Mrs. Hatch immediately after I saw you,‚Äù she said, as though continuing her confession.
‚ÄúYes‚ÅÝ‚Äîyes; I know,‚Äù he assented, with a rising tinge of embarrassment.

‚ÄúAnd that I did so because you told me to. Before you came I had already begun to see that it would be impossible to remain with her‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor the reasons you gave me; but I wouldn‚Äôt admit it‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI wouldn‚Äôt let you see that I understood what you meant.‚Äù
‚ÄúAh, I might have trusted you to find your own way out‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt overwhelm me with the sense of my officiousness!‚Äù

His light tone, in which, had her nerves been steadier, she would have recognized the mere effort to bridge over an awkward moment, jarred on her passionate desire to be understood. In her strange state of extra-lucidity, which gave her the sense of being already at the heart of the situation, it seemed incredible that anyone should think it necessary to linger in the conventional outskirts of wordplay and evasion.
‚ÄúIt was not that‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI was not ungrateful,‚Äù she insisted. But the power of expression failed her suddenly; she felt a tremor in her throat, and two tears gathered and fell slowly from her eyes.
Selden moved forward and took her hand. ‚ÄúYou are very tired. Why won‚Äôt you sit down and let me make you comfortable?‚Äù
He drew her to the armchair near the fire, and placed a cushion behind her shoulders.
‚ÄúAnd now you must let me make you some tea: you know I always have that amount of hospitality at my command.‚Äù
She shook her head, and two more tears ran over. But she did not weep easily, and the long habit of self-control reasserted itself, though she was still too tremulous to speak.
‚ÄúYou know I can coax the water to boil in five minutes,‚Äù Selden continued, speaking as though she were a troubled child.

His words recalled the vision of that other afternoon when they had sat together over his tea-table and talked jestingly of her future. There were moments when that day seemed more remote than any other event in her life; and yet she could always relive it in its minutest detail.
She made a gesture of refusal. ‚ÄúNo: I drink too much tea. I would rather sit quiet‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI must go in a moment,‚Äù she added confusedly.

Selden continued to stand near her, leaning against the mantelpiece. The tinge of constraint was beginning to be more distinctly perceptible under the friendly ease of his manner. Her self-absorption had not allowed her to perceive it at first; but now that her consciousness was once more putting forth its eager feelers, she saw that her presence was becoming an embarrassment to him. Such a situation can be saved only by an immediate outrush of feeling; and on Selden‚Äôs side the determining impulse was still lacking.

The discovery did not disturb Lily as it might once have done. She had passed beyond the phase of well-bred reciprocity, in which every demonstration must be scrupulously proportioned to the emotion it elicits, and generosity of feeling is the only ostentation condemned. But the sense of loneliness returned with redoubled force as she saw herself forever shut out from Selden‚Äôs inmost self. She had come to him with no definite purpose; the mere longing to see him had directed her; but the secret hope she had carried with her suddenly revealed itself in its death-pang.

‚ÄúI must go,‚Äù she repeated, making a motion to rise from her chair. ‚ÄúBut I may not see you again for a long time, and I wanted to tell you that I have never forgotten the things you said to me at Bellomont, and that sometimes‚ÅÝ‚Äîsometimes when I seemed farthest from remembering them‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey have helped me, and kept me from mistakes; kept me from really becoming what many people have thought me.‚Äù

Strive as she would to put some order in her thoughts, the words would not come more clearly; yet she felt that she could not leave him without trying to make him understand that she had saved herself whole from the seeming ruin of her life.
A change had come over Selden‚Äôs face as she spoke. Its guarded look had yielded to an expression still untinged by personal emotion, but full of a gentle understanding.

‚ÄúI am glad to have you tell me that; but nothing I have said has really made the difference. The difference is in yourself‚ÅÝ‚Äîit will always be there. And since it is there, it can‚Äôt really matter to you what people think: you are so sure that your friends will always understand you.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, don‚Äôt say that‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt say that what you have told me has made no difference. It seems to shut me out‚ÅÝ‚Äîto leave me all alone with the other people.‚Äù She had risen and stood before him, once more completely mastered by the inner urgency of the moment. The consciousness of his half-divined reluctance had vanished. Whether he wished it or not, he must see her wholly for once before they parted.

Her voice had gathered strength, and she looked him gravely in the eyes as she continued. ‚ÄúOnce‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwice‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou gave me the chance to escape from my life, and I refused it: refused it because I was a coward. Afterward I saw my mistake‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI saw I could never be happy with what had contented me before. But it was too late: you had judged me‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI understood. It was too late for happiness‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut not too late to be helped by the thought of what I had missed. That is all I have lived on‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt take it from me now! Even in my worst moments it has been like a little light in the darkness. Some women are strong enough to be good by themselves, but I needed the help of your belief in me. Perhaps I might have resisted a great temptation, but the little ones would have pulled me down. And then I remembered‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI remembered your saying that such a life could never satisfy me; and I was ashamed to admit to myself that it could. That is what you did for me‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat is what I wanted to thank you for. I wanted to tell you that I have always remembered; and that I have tried‚ÅÝ‚Äîtried hard‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

She broke off suddenly. Her tears had risen again, and in drawing out her handkerchief her fingers touched the packet in the folds of her dress. A wave of colour suffused her, and the words died on her lips. Then she lifted her eyes to his and went on in an altered voice.

‚ÄúI have tried hard‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut life is difficult, and I am a very useless person. I can hardly be said to have an independent existence. I was just a screw or a cog in the great machine I called life, and when I dropped out of it I found I was of no use anywhere else. What can one do when one finds that one only fits into one hole? One must get back to it or be thrown out into the rubbish heap‚ÅÝ‚Äîand you don‚Äôt know what it‚Äôs like in the rubbish heap!‚Äù

Her lips wavered into a smile‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe had been distracted by the whimsical remembrance of the confidences she had made to him, two years earlier, in that very room. Then she had been planning to marry Percy Gryce‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat was it she was planning now?
The blood had risen strongly under Selden‚Äôs dark skin, but his emotion showed itself only in an added seriousness of manner.
‚ÄúYou have something to tell me‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you mean to marry?‚Äù he said abruptly.

Lily‚Äôs eyes did not falter, but a look of wonder, of puzzled self-interrogation, formed itself slowly in their depths. In the light of his question, she had paused to ask herself if her decision had really been taken when she entered the room.
‚ÄúYou always told me I should have to come to it sooner or later!‚Äù she said with a faint smile.
‚ÄúAnd you have come to it now?‚Äù

‚ÄúI shall have to come to it‚ÅÝ‚Äîpresently. But there is something else I must come to first.‚Äù She paused again, trying to transmit to her voice the steadiness of her recovered smile. ‚ÄúThere is someone I must say goodbye to. Oh, not you‚ÅÝ‚Äîwe are sure to see each other again‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut the Lily Bart you knew. I have kept her with me all this time, but now we are going to part, and I have brought her back to you‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI am going to leave her here. When I go out presently she will not go with me. I shall like to think that she has stayed with you‚ÅÝ‚Äîand she‚Äôll be no trouble, she‚Äôll take up no room.‚Äù
She went toward him, and put out her hand, still smiling. ‚ÄúWill you let her stay with you?‚Äù she asked.
He caught her hand, and she felt in his the vibration of feeling that had not yet risen to his lips. ‚ÄúLily‚ÅÝ‚Äîcan‚Äôt I help you?‚Äù he exclaimed.

She looked at him gently. ‚ÄúDo you remember what you said to me once? That you could help me only by loving me? Well‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou did love me for a moment; and it helped me. It has always helped me. But the moment is gone‚ÅÝ‚Äîit was I who let it go. And one must go on living. Goodbye.‚Äù

She laid her other hand on his, and they looked at each other with a kind of solemnity, as though they stood in the presence of death. Something in truth lay dead between them‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe love she had killed in him and could no longer call to life. But something lived between them also, and leaped up in her like an imperishable flame: it was the love his love had kindled, the passion of her soul for his.

In its light everything else dwindled and fell away from her. She understood now that she could not go forth and leave her old self with him: that self must indeed live on in his presence, but it must still continue to be hers.

Selden had retained her hand, and continued to scrutinize her with a strange sense of foreboding. The external aspect of the situation had vanished for him as completely as for her: he felt it only as one of those rare moments which lift the veil from their faces as they pass.

‚ÄúLily,‚Äù he said in a low voice, ‚Äúyou mustn‚Äôt speak in this way. I can‚Äôt let you go without knowing what you mean to do. Things may change‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut they don‚Äôt pass. You can never go out of my life.‚Äù
She met his eyes with an illumined look. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúI see that now. Let us always be friends. Then I shall feel safe, whatever happens.‚Äù
‚ÄúWhatever happens? What do you mean? What is going to happen?‚Äù
She turned away quietly and walked toward the hearth.
‚ÄúNothing at present‚ÅÝ‚Äîexcept that I am very cold, and that before I go you must make up the fire for me.‚Äù

She knelt on the hearthrug, stretching her hands to the embers. Puzzled by the sudden change in her tone, he mechanically gathered a handful of wood from the basket and tossed it on the fire. As he did so, he noticed how thin her hands looked against the rising light of the flames. He saw too, under the loose lines of her dress, how the curves of her figure had shrunk to angularity; he remembered long afterward how the red play of the flame sharpened the depression of her nostrils, and intensified the blackness of the shadows which struck up from her cheekbones to her eyes. She knelt there for a few moments in silence; a silence which he dared not break. When she rose he fancied that he saw her draw something from her dress and drop it into the fire; but he hardly noticed the gesture at the time. His faculties seemed tranced, and he was still groping for the word to break the spell.
She went up to him and laid her hands on his shoulders. ‚ÄúGoodbye,‚Äù she said, and as he bent over her she touched his forehead with her lips.
